statement.p: By your cell phone contract, you pay a monthly fee plus {\$0.03} for each minute you spend on the phone. In one month, you spent 270 minutes over the phone, and had a bill totaling {\$21.10}.

Let x be the number of minutes you spend on the phone in a month, and let y be your total cell phone bill for that month, in dollars. Use a linear equation to model your monthly bill based on the number of minutes you spend on the phone.
A point-slope equation to model this is .
If you spend 130 minutes on the phone in a month, you would be billed .
If your bill was {\$27.10} one month, you must have spent minutes on the phone in that month.

li.p: A lines equation in point-slope form looks like y=m(x-x_0)+y_0, where m is the slope, and (x_0,y_0) is some point we know to be on the line. We first need to find the lines slope. The slope of the linear model has been given to us: {\$0.03} per minute.
So now we have that y=0.03(x-x_0)+y_0. We can use (x_0,y_0)=(270,21.1) to get {y = 0.03\mathopen{}\left(x-270\right)+21.1}.
In one month, you spent 130 minutes on the phone. To find out the bill, we substitute 130 in for x in the equation y=0.03(x-270)+21.1, and we have:
If you spent 130 minutes over the phone in a month, your bill for that month would be {\$16.90}.
In one month, you had a cell phone bill of {\$27.10}. To find how many minutes you spent on the phone that month, we substitute 27.1 in for y in the equation y=0.03(x-270)+21.1, and we have:
If your bill was {\$27.10} in one month, you must have spent 470 minutes on the phone.
